result:
The cursor blinks and I think about how strange it is to be asked to write whatever comes. There's a freedom in it that feels almost suspicious, like being handed an empty room and told to make yourself at home. What does come, when nothing is required?

I think about windows. Not the metaphorical kind but actual windows, the way light falls through them at different hours and changes the character of a room without changing anything else. A kitchen at 7am is not the same kitchen at 4pm even when every object sits exactly where it sat. The light does something. We are like rooms in that way, I think—mostly the same furniture, mostly the same walls, but the light shifts and we become unrecognizable to ourselves.

I think about how people say "I lost track of time" as though time were a small dog that wandered off. The phrase admits something we don't usually say plainly: that attention is what makes time exist for us at all. When you're absorbed, hours collapse. When you're waiting, minutes inflate. The clock keeps its steady arithmetic, but we live in a different country, one with rubber distances.

There's a particular kind of silence in libraries that I find myself wanting to describe. It isn't the absence of sound—libraries are full of small sounds, pages turning, throats clearing, the hum of fluorescents. It's a silence made of agreement. Everyone in the room has decided, together, to be quiet, and that mutual decision creates something thicker than mere quiet. You can feel it when you walk in. The same is true of churches, of certain forests, of the moment before a concert begins. Silence that people make together is different from silence that just happens.

I keep thinking about the word "anyway." It's a small workhorse of a word. People use it to climb out of a digression, or to acknowledge that they've been complaining and want to move on, or to signal that despite everything, life continues. "Anyway." It contains a small shrug, a small forgiveness, a small refusal to stay stuck. I admire it.

What else comes. I think about the experience of forgetting a word that you know perfectly well. It sits behind a closed door in your mind. You can feel its shape, its number of syllables, the consonant it starts with, but the word itself refuses to come out. And then, hours later, while you're doing something completely unrelated—washing a dish, tying a shoe—the word arrives like a cat returning from wherever cats go. There's something humbling about this. We are not in charge of our own minds in the way we like to pretend.

I think about how strange it is that we sleep. A third of life spent unconscious, hallucinating. If you described sleep to someone who had never experienced it, they would not believe you. "You go where? You see what? And you do this every night?" It would sound like a religious ritual or a mild psychosis. But because everyone does it, we treat it as ordinary. Almost everything about being alive is like this—astonishing if you could see it fresh, invisible because you can't.

I think about the smell of rain on hot pavement, which has a name—petrichor—and the way naming a thing both honors it and slightly diminishes it. Before you knew the word, the smell was wild. After, it's classified. Something is gained and something is lost in every act of naming.

I think about the people who built the chair you're sitting in, or the keyboard I'm using, or the road outside the window of whoever reads this. Whole lives went into the things we touch without thinking. A chain of hands stretches back behind every object. We are surrounded constantly by the labor of strangers, most of whom we will never meet, many of whom are no longer alive. The world is denser with other people than we usually let ourselves notice.

I think about how the best conversations have a particular quality of mutual surprise—neither person quite knew what they thought until they said it, and the saying of it discovered something. This is different from the kind of talking where two people exchange already-formed opinions. The good kind makes both people slightly different by the end. You can feel when it's happening. The air gets a little electric.

I think about endings, since I can sense this one coming. Endings are difficult because they have to do two contradictory things: they have to wrap up and they have to release. Too much wrapping and they feel forced. Too much release and they feel abandoned. The best endings do something a little sideways—they don't close the thing so much as set it gently down and step away, leaving the reader holding something they didn't quite expect to be holding.

I notice I'm near the limit. There's something pleasant about a constraint announcing itself. Without the thousand-word frame, I could keep going or stop anywhere; with it, the shape becomes visible. Constraints are generous in this way. They give you walls to push against, which is more useful than infinite space.

So: here is the small thing I've made out of an empty room and a thousand words. Windows, silence, the word anyway, the cat-like return of forgotten words, the strangeness of sleep, petrichor, the chain of strangers behind every object, conversations that surprise their own speakers, and endings that set things down gently. I don't know if any of it connects, but I notice it's all roughly about attention—about what becomes visible when you stop and look. Maybe that's what comes, when nothing is required. Just the things that were already there.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
